title: MATH 3190 Homework 10
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_10 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

Problem 1 (45 points)
In the itsmr package is the dataset called wine that contains 142 accounts of Australian red wine sales per month from January 1980 to October 1991.

Part a (5 points)
Load in this data and convert it to a tsibble. You can do this by first making it a ts object by typing ts(wine, deltat = 1/12, start = 1980) and then piping that into the as_tsibble() function in the tsibble package.
Then use ggplot() to plot the time series. Show both the points and lines connecting them. On the \(x\) axis, put each year using the scale_x_yearmonth() function and give the plot a title.

library(itsmr)
library(tsibble)


Attaching package: 'tsibble'


The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    intersect, setdiff, union

library(ggplot2)
library(feasts)

Loading required package: fabletools



Attaching package: 'fabletools'


The following object is masked from 'package:itsmr':

    forecast

library(dplyr)


Attaching package: 'dplyr'


The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    filter, lag


The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

wine_tsibble <- ts(wine, deltat = 1/12, start = 1980) |>
  as_tsibble() |>
  rename(year = index, sales = value)

ggplot(wine_tsibble, aes(x = year, y = sales)) +
  geom_line(size = 1) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_x_yearmonth(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") +
  labs(title = "Australian Red Wine Sales (Jan 1980 - Oct 1991)", x = "Year", y = "Sales") +
  theme(axis.title = element_text(size = 12),
        plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"))

Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.











Part b (2 points)
Remove the last 34 rows from the wine data. We will use them to compare how our forecasts look later.

wine_train <- wine_tsibble |>
  slice(1:(n() - 34))



Part c (5 points)
Use the gg_season() plot in the ggtime library to plot the seasonality. Does it seem like there is trend and/or seasonality in this time series? If so, explain the patterns.

wine_train |>
  gg_season(sales) + labs(title = "Seasonal Plot: Australian Red Wine Sales", y = "Sales")

Warning: `gg_season()` was deprecated in feasts 0.4.2.
ℹ Please use `ggtime::gg_season()` instead.
ℹ Graphics functions have been moved to the {ggtime} package. Please use
  `library(ggtime)` instead.









There is clearly a trend here, more wine is sold during the summer months. June, July, and August nearly all have the most amount of red wine sold for each year.


Part d (4 points)
Use the adf.test() function in the tseries library to perform a Dickey-Fuller test to determine if we have evidence this time series is stationary. Only the column with the sales should be entered. Since we have data by month, use the k = 12 option. Comment on the results of the test.

library(tseries)

Registered S3 method overwritten by 'quantmod':
  method            from
  as.zoo.data.frame zoo 



Attaching package: 'tseries'


The following object is masked from 'package:itsmr':

    arma

adf <- adf.test(wine_train$sales, k = 12)

print(adf)


    Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test

data:  wine_train$sales
Dickey-Fuller = -1.6093, Lag order = 12, p-value = 0.7387
alternative hypothesis: stationary


The p-value is 0.7387, which is a lot higher than the usual significance level of 0.05. This gives strong evidence that the data is non-stationary. This aligns with the upward trend and seasonality that was observed in the plots.


Part e (6 points)
Difference the data once. Take \(Y_t = Y_{t+1} - Y_{t}\) for \(t=1,\dots,n-1\). In the notes, I defined the differences as \(Y_t = Y_{t} - Y_{t+1}\), but it actually does not matter. Plot this differenced time series (you can just put the index on the x-axis) and use the adf.test() function again on the differenced data. Again, use k = 12 option. Comment on the results of the test.

wine_diff <- wine_train |>
  mutate(diff_sales = difference(sales, lag = 1)) |>
  filter(!is.na(diff_sales))

ggplot(wine_diff, aes(x = year, y = diff_sales)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  labs(title = "First-Order Differenced Wine Sales",
       x = "Date",
       y = "Change in Sales (Y_t - Y_{t-1}") +
  theme_minimal()







adf_diff <- adf.test(wine_diff$diff_sales, k = 12)

Warning in adf.test(wine_diff$diff_sales, k = 12): p-value smaller than printed
p-value


The test result here shows a very small p-value, less than 0.05. This means the differenced data is now stationary.


Part f (4 points)
Now use the auto.arima() function in the forecast library to fit an ARIMA model to this time series (put the entire original tsibble into this function, not the differences time series).
Feel free to check out this resource that discusses more about time series, including what the drift term is.

library(forecast)


Attaching package: 'forecast'


The following object is masked from 'package:itsmr':

    forecast

wine_fit <- auto.arima(wine_train)



Part g (5 points)
Use the forecast() function from the forecast library to predict the future for 34 months. You should specify you are using the forecast library by typing forecast::forecast() since the itsmr library also has a forecast() function. The input of this function is your model you fit in part (f). Use the model fit to the undifferenced data.
Then use the autoplot() function to plot the forecast.

wine_forecast <- forecast::forecast(wine_fit, h = 34, level = 95)

autoplot(wine_forecast)










Part h (8 points)
Plot the actual values for 1989, 1990, and 1991 along with the predicted values from the forecast and a shaded 95% interval bands. The geom_ribbon() function may be useful here. Is there any point where the true value is outside the interval? If so, report the year(s) and month(s) where that is the case.

wine_test <- tail(wine_tsibble, 34)

forecast_df <- data.frame(
  year  = wine_test$year,
  sales = as.numeric(wine_forecast$mean), 
  lower = as.numeric(wine_forecast$lower),
  upper = as.numeric(wine_forecast$upper)
)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = wine_test, aes(x = year, y = sales, color = "Actual"), size = 1) +
  geom_line(data = forecast_df, aes(x = year, y = sales, color = "Forecast"), linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_ribbon(data = forecast_df, aes(x = year, ymin = lower, ymax = upper), fill = "magenta", alpha = 0.2) +
  scale_x_yearmonth(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") +
  labs(title = "Actual vs. Forecasted Wine Sales",
       x = "Year", y = "Sales") +
  theme_minimal()







out_check <- wine_test$sales < forecast_df$lower | wine_test$sales > forecast_df$upper

wine_test[out_check, ]

# A tsibble: 1 x 2 [1M]
      year sales
     <mth> <dbl>
1 1991 Apr  1525


There is one notable time where the actual value is outside the confidence intervals. This is April 1991.


Part i (6 points)
Use the checkresiduals() function in the forecast library to create a residual plot, an autocorrelation plot, and a histogram for the residuals. The input to this function should be your model fit with the auto.arima() function. Comment on whether the assumptions seem to be satisfied here. If you’d like, you can make a QQ-plot as well, but the histogram contains the same information.
Note: this function will also give you information from the Ljung-Box test, which tests if the residuals exhibit autocorrelation. A large p-value means we have no evidence of autocorrelation.

checkresiduals(wine_fit)









    Ljung-Box test

data:  Residuals from ARIMA(1,0,1)(0,1,1)[12] with drift
Q* = 19.463, df = 19, p-value = 0.4275

Model df: 3.   Total lags used: 22


The residual plot looks good. There’s no clear patterns. The ACF plot also looks good, and there’s no major autocorrelation in the residuals. All of the lines fall in between the blue dotted lines. The histogram isn’t as good. It is skewed to the right, which means the normality assumption for the residuals might be violated which would then affect the coefficient fit.

title: MATH 3190 Homework 2
text:
Part e (11 points)
Many of these queries are generating outputs that are fairly small. That is, they are easily able to be stored as variables in R. A couple hundred thousand rows is fine to work with since that only takes a couple MB of RAM.

Using the dbGetQuery() function again, obtain the arrival delay times and the name of the airline (by joining with the airlines table) for all the data entries that have a delay time more than 60 minutes. Save this output as a tibble in R.
Once you have this, make side-by-side boxplots in ggplot() for the arrival delay time split by the airline. This plot won’t look too great unless the y-axis is scaled. Go ahead and use the log10 scale like we did back in Lab 2. Then look up how to angle the axis labels so they do not overlap and incorporate that in your plot.


library(DBI)
library(RSQLite)
library(tibble)

combined_flight <- dbConnect(SQLite(), "/Users/janewillden/Downloads/combined.sqlite")

delay_data <- dbGetQuery(combined_flight, "SELECT
                            f.ARRIVAL_DELAY,
                            a.AIRLINE AS airline_name
                         FROM flights f
                         INNER JOIN airlines a
                            ON f.AIRLINE = a.IATA_CODE
                         WHERE f.ARRIVAL_DELAY > 60;") |>
  as_tibble()


Part f (10 points)

Now, let’s NOT use the dbGetQuery() function. Instead, insert an SQL code chunk for this part of the problem that selects the average arrival delay (for some reason, there is not a median function built in to SQL) and the airline’s full name for each airline. So, this output should only have 14 rows.
Then pass this output back to R and create a barplot with the airlines on the x-axis and the average arrival delay on the y-axis. Again, angle the x-axis labels so they do not overlap.



SELECT
  a.AIRLINE AS airline_name,
  AVG(f.ARRIVAL_DELAY) AS avg_arrival_delay
FROM flights f
INNER JOIN airlines a
  ON f.AIRLINE = a.IATA_CODE
GROUP BY a.AIRLINE;

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(avg_delay_airline,
       aes(x = airline_name, y = avg_arrival_delay)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(
    x = "Airline",
    y = "Average Arrival Delay (minutes)",
    title = "Average Arrival Delay by Airline"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )



Part g (12 points)

Now let’s use the dbplyr add-on to the dplyr package to work with an SQL database directly. You may have to install the dbplyr package. Use the tbl() function to create an object you can work with directly.


library(DBI)
library(RSQLite)
library(dplyr)


Attaching package: 'dplyr'


The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    filter, lag


The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

library(dbplyr)


Attaching package: 'dbplyr'


The following objects are masked from 'package:dplyr':

    ident, sql

library(ggplot2)

combined_flight <- dbConnect(SQLite(), "/Users/janewillden/Downloads/combined.sqlite")

flights_tbl <- tbl(combined_flight, "flights")

airlines_tbl <- tbl(combined_flight, "airlines")


With that object, using R commands from the tidyverse, find the average and standard deviation of the elapsed time of the flights by airline and arrange it by the SD (with the largest at the top). Make sure to use the inner_join() function so that the name of the airline, not just its code, is given. Note that to save this object in R’s memory, you will need to do something like pipe it into the collect() function.


summary_stats <- flights_tbl %>%
  group_by(AIRLINE) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_elapsed_time = mean(ELAPSED_TIME, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_elapsed_time = sd(ELAPSED_TIME, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  inner_join(airlines_tbl, by = c("AIRLINE" = "IATA_CODE")) %>%
  arrange(desc(sd_elapsed_time)) %>%
  collect()


Finally, make a ggplot bar graph like the one you made in Part f, but use the standard deviation as the heights on the y-axis. Why do you suppose the airline with the largest standard deviation in elapsed time has such variable flight times?


ggplot(summary_stats, aes(x = AIRLINE, y = sd_elapsed_time)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(x = "Airline",
      y = "Standard Deviation of Elapsed Time (minutes)",
      title = "Flight Time Variability by Airline") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))








Finally, when you are done with this assignment, it is good practice to close the connection to the flights database.

dbDisconnect(combined_flight)

title: MATH 3190 Homework 7
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_7 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

Problem 1 (57 points)
In Homework 6, we briefly looked at a small sample from this data set. Now we have the full data set with 20,640 districts. For districts in California from 1990, we want to predict the median house value for the district based on the district location (longitude and latitude), the median house age in the block group, the total number of rooms in the homes, the total number of bedrooms, the population in the district, the number of households, and the median income (in $10,000). These data were obtained from scikit-learn.org and can be found in the Data folder of the Math3190_Sp26 GitHub repo with the name cal_housing.data.

Part a (5 points)
Read in the data. The read.csv() function works for files that end in .data if they are comma separated like this file is. However, be aware that the housing data does not have column names, so make sure to put header = FALSE. Assign appropriate column names to the columns and then take logs (base e) of all variables except lat and long. Once this is done, split the data into training and testing sets with the testing containing 20% of the values. Set a seed when you do this.

library(dplyr) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
library(caret) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
set.seed(3190)

housing_data <- read.csv("/Users/janewillden/Downloads/cal_housing.data", header = FALSE)

colnames(housing_data) <- c("longitude", "latitude", "median_house_age", "total_rooms", "total_bedrooms", "population", "households", "median_income", "median_house_value")

housing_log <- housing_data |>
  mutate(logage = log(median_house_age),
         logrooms = log(total_rooms),
         logbedrooms = log(total_bedrooms),
         logpop = log(population),
         loghouseholds = log(households),
         logincome = log(median_income),
         loghousevalue = log(median_house_value))

train_index <- createDataPartition(housing_log$loghousevalue, p = 0.8, list = FALSE)
housing_train <- housing_log[train_index, ]
housing_test <- housing_log[-train_index, ]



Part b (5 points)
Use the following command to view pair-wise scatter plots for the data here. Change housing_train to whatever you called the training set and be sure to change the eval option for the code chunk to true. Note, that lower option changes the points to be plotted with periods instead of circles. This speeds up plotting time considerably and makes the plots more readable since there are many data points here.
Based on these plots, does multicollinearity appear to be an issue here? Explain.

library(GGally)
ggpairs(housing_train, 
        lower = list(continuous = wrap("points", shape = "."))
)








Multicollinearity does seem to be an issue here. There is multiple pairs of predictors that seem to form linear patterns, which represents strong correlations between the variables. The plots where the points cluster closely along a line suggest that one variable can be predicted fairly easily from the other, which is an indication of multicollinearity.


Part c (4 points)
Fit a OLS regression model predicting house prices from all other variables. Check its summary output and its VIF values. Comment on the VIFs.

library(car) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()

ols_fit <- lm(loghousevalue ~ logage + logrooms + logbedrooms + logpop + loghouseholds + logincome, data = housing_train)

summary(ols_fit)


Call:
lm(formula = loghousevalue ~ logage + logrooms + logbedrooms + 
    logpop + loghouseholds + logincome, data = housing_train)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-3.1173 -0.2275  0.0048  0.2216  3.3334 

Coefficients:
               Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)   11.549812   0.047237  244.51   <2e-16 ***
logage         0.149831   0.005501   27.24   <2e-16 ***
logrooms      -0.737185   0.017758  -41.51   <2e-16 ***
logbedrooms    0.681499   0.024184   28.18   <2e-16 ***
logpop        -0.358066   0.011170  -32.06   <2e-16 ***
loghouseholds  0.450331   0.021876   20.59   <2e-16 ***
logincome      1.103520   0.009141  120.72   <2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.3737 on 16506 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:   0.57, Adjusted R-squared:  0.5698 
F-statistic:  3646 on 6 and 16506 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

vif_tbl <- tibble(Predictor = names(vif(ols_fit)), VIF = round(vif(ols_fit), 2))
print(vif_tbl)

# A tibble: 6 × 2
  Predictor       VIF
  <chr>         <dbl>
1 logage         1.14
2 logrooms      20.8 
3 logbedrooms   36.7 
4 logpop         7.96
5 loghouseholds 30.0 
6 logincome      2.17


The VIF shows that three predictors are highly collinear, and these are logrooms, logbedrooms, and loghouseholds. They all are greater than 10. On the other hand, logage and logincome have low VIFs which means they are mostly independent of other variables.


Part d (3 points)
Fit a principal component model using the pcr() function in the pls package. Since the variables are on very different scales, use the scale = TRUE option in the function. Then find the VIF values for this model. The vif() function from the car library won’t work here. Instead, you can take the diagonals of the inverse of the correlation matrix (like is shown in the regularization section of Notes 7) for the scores output. The code for this is diag(solve(cor(pca_model$scores))). Of course, you need to change pca_model to what you called your model.

library(pls) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()

housing_pcr <- pcr(loghousevalue ~ logage + logrooms + logbedrooms + logpop + loghouseholds + logincome, data = housing_train, scale = TRUE)

scores_mat <- housing_pcr$scores[, 1:6]
vif_pcr <- diag(solve(cor(scores_mat)))

tibble(PC = paste0("PC", 1:6), VIF = round(vif_pcr, 3))

# A tibble: 6 × 2
  PC      VIF
  <chr> <dbl>
1 PC1       1
2 PC2       1
3 PC3       1
4 PC4       1
5 PC5       1
6 PC6       1




Part e (5 points)
Now take the summary of your principal component model. With PCA, the common amount of variation we want to explain in the predictors is 90% or more. How many components are needed to achieve this? For that number of components, how much of the variation in log of home values is explained? How many components are needed to explain a “good” amount of the variation in the log of home values? Note: the upper bound on the amount of variation in the log of home values here with all 8 components will be equal to the \(R^2\) value of the OLS model.

explained_prop <- explvar(housing_pcr) / 100
total_explained <- cumsum(explained_prop)

tibble(PC = seq_along(total_explained), TotalVar = round(total_explained, 3)) |> print()

# A tibble: 6 × 2
     PC TotalVar
  <int>    <dbl>
1     1    0.647
2     2    0.83 
3     3    0.969
4     4    0.991
5     5    0.997
6     6    1    


Three components are needed to explain at least 90% of the variance – after PC 3, 96.9% of the variance is explained. A large number of componenets are needed to explain a good amount of variation in the log of home values.


Part f (4 points)
Now fit a partial least squares model using the plsr() function in the pls package with scale = TRUE. Then find the VIF values for this model like you did in part (d) and comment on them.

housing_pls <- plsr(loghousevalue ~ logage + logrooms + logbedrooms + logpop + loghouseholds + logincome, data = housing_train, scale = TRUE, validation = "CV", segments = 10)

opt_ncomp <- selectNcomp(housing_pls, method = "onesigma")
cat("Selected components:", opt_ncomp, "\n")

Selected components: 5 

scores_mat <- housing_pls$scores[, 1:opt_ncomp, drop = FALSE]

vif_pls <- diag(solve(cor(scores_mat)))
tibble(PC = paste0("PC", 1:opt_ncomp),
       VIF = round(vif_pls, 3)) |> print()

# A tibble: 5 × 2
  PC      VIF
  <chr> <dbl>
1 PC1       1
2 PC2       1
3 PC3       1
4 PC4       1
5 PC5       1


The VIFs for the PLS compoenents are all one which means that each component is uncorrelated with the others. There is no multicollinearity among them, which is different from the OLS model.


Part g (4 points)
Now take the summary of your partial least squares model. Compare this output to the summary of the PCA model. Explain what the differences are.
How many components are needed to balance a good amount of variation explained in \(X\) and in \(y\) for the PLS model?

summary(housing_pls)

Data:   X dimension: 16513 6 
    Y dimension: 16513 1
Fit method: kernelpls
Number of components considered: 6

VALIDATION: RMSEP
Cross-validated using 10 random segments.
       (Intercept)  1 comps  2 comps  3 comps  4 comps  5 comps  6 comps
CV          0.5697   0.4491   0.4102   0.4044   0.3820   0.3749   0.3741
adjCV       0.5697   0.4491   0.4102   0.4044   0.3819   0.3749   0.3741

TRAINING: % variance explained
               1 comps  2 comps  3 comps  4 comps  5 comps  6 comps
X                34.86    81.01    96.22    98.19    99.72      100
loghousevalue    37.92    48.20    49.67    55.16    56.81       57


The PCA summary explains variation in the predictors, while the PLS summary explains both the predictors and the response. About 4 components are needed in the PLS model to balance explaining variation in both the predictors and the response while keeping prediction error low.


Part h (8 points)
In part (e), you said how many components were needed for the PCA to explain a good amount of the \(Y\) variable. Using that number of components, use the PCA and PLS models to find the root MSE when predicting the testing set. Note: the root MSE (or residual standard error, also abbreviated RMSE) is found by taking the square root of the sum of squared residuals divided by the residual degrees of freedom, which is \(n-p-1\), where \(p\) is the number of predictors in the model. Also, be aware that when you use the predict() function on your model, it will give you predictions for each number of components. You can access the predictions for using 3 components, for example, by typing predict(pca_model, test_set)[,,3].
Then in part (g), you said how many components were needed for the PLS Using that number of components. Using this number of components, use the PCA and PLS models to find the root MSE predicting the testing set.
Compare the results of the predictions in both cases.

pca_ncomp <- 3
pls_ncomp <- 4

pred_pca_all <- predict(housing_pcr, newdata = housing_test, ncomp = pca_ncomp)
pred_pca <- as.vector(pred_pca_all)

pred_pls_all <- predict(housing_pls, newdata = housing_test, ncomp = pls_ncomp)
pred_pls <- as.vector(pred_pls_all)
n_test <- nrow(housing_test)

rmse_custom <- function(y_obs, y_pred, p){rss   <- sum((y_obs - y_pred)^2)
  df <- n_test - p - 1
  sqrt(rss / df)}

rmse_pca <- rmse_custom(housing_test$loghousevalue, pred_pca, p = pca_ncomp)
rmse_pls <- rmse_custom(housing_test$loghousevalue, pred_pls, p = pls_ncomp)

tibble(
  Model = c("PCA (PCR)", "PLS"),
  Components = c(pca_ncomp, pls_ncomp),
  RMSE_log = c(round(rmse_pca, 4), round(rmse_pls, 4)),
  RMSE_original = c(round(exp(rmse_pca) - 1, 2), round(exp(rmse_pls) - 1, 2)) ) |> print()

# A tibble: 2 × 4
  Model     Components RMSE_log RMSE_original
  <chr>          <dbl>    <dbl>         <dbl>
1 PCA (PCR)          3    0.412          0.51
2 PLS                4    0.378          0.46


The test-set RMSE for the 3 comp PCR model is 0.412, while the 4 comp PLS model has a lower RMSE of 0.378. This means the PLS model reduces the prediction error.


Part i (12 points)
Using the number of components you said were needed for the PLS model in part (g), come up with reasonable surrogates for each of those components. Look at the projection output for this. When creating your surrogates, make sure to center and scale each variable. You can do this with the scale() function and putting your housing data frame or tibble in it.
Then fit a OLS model using those surrogates in the training set. Check the VIF of this model to make sure each one is below 5. If they are not, your surrogates should be changed. You can use the vif() function in the car package here.

X_raw <- housing_train |> 
  select(logage, logrooms, logbedrooms, logpop, loghouseholds, logincome)

X_scaled <- scale(X_raw)

load_mat <- housing_pls$loading.weights[, 1:pls_ncomp]

surrogates_mat <- X_scaled %*% load_mat

surrogates_tbl <- as_tibble(surrogates_mat, .name_repair = ~ paste0("Surrogate", 1:pls_ncomp))

train_with_surrogates <- housing_train |> bind_cols(surrogates_tbl)

ols_surrogate <- lm(loghousevalue ~ .,data = train_with_surrogates |> select(loghousevalue, starts_with("Surrogate")))

vif_vals <- vif(ols_surrogate)

The surrogates were constructed as linear combinations of the scaled predictors using the PLS loading weights, which represent the PLS components. The VIF values for all surrogate variables are below 5, suggesting that multicollinearity isn’t an issue in this model.


Part j (7 points)
Now using that model you fit in the previous part with the surrogates, find the root MSE for predicting the testing set using that model, and compare it to what you got in part (h). Describe some pros and cons of the surrogate model.
Hint: to predict using this model, you will have to create a newdata data frame (or tibble) and then redefine your surrogates in that data frame using the scaled testing data set. Make sure the variable names in the newdata data frame match the variables names used in the model you defined in part (i).

X_test_raw <- housing_test |> 
  select(logage, logrooms, logbedrooms, logpop, loghouseholds, logincome)

X_test_scaled <- scale(X_test_raw, center = attr(X_scaled, "scaled:center"), scale  = attr(X_scaled, "scaled:scale"))

surrogates_test <- X_test_scaled %*% load_mat

surrogates_test_tbl <- as_tibble(surrogates_test, .name_repair = ~ paste0("Surrogate", 1:pls_ncomp))

pred_surrogate <- predict(ols_surrogate, newdata = surrogates_test_tbl)

rmse_surrogate <- rmse_custom(housing_test$loghousevalue, pred_surrogate, p = pls_ncomp)

Pros: low VIF – removes multicollinearity, can use standard OLS tools Cons: Less interpretable than original variables, requires extra preprocessing The RMSE of the surrogate model is very similar to the PLS model and lower than the PCR model, which suggests that it retains most of the predictive power of PLS.



Problem 2 (33 points)
In base R, there is a dataset called penguins. Type ?penguins to read a little about the data.

Part a (6 points)
Remove the rows in the penguins dataset that have missing values. Then use the ggpairs() function from GGally to make pairwise scatterplots for the four quantitative variables and color the points based on the species variable. Note that the ggpairs() function has a columns argument where you can select the columns you want to plot and it has a mapping argument where you can put aes(color = species, alpha = 0.5) to color the points by species.
Include the plot here and then comment on which scatterplots seem to nicely separate the penguins by species and which tend to have worse separation.

library(ggplot2)
library(GGally)
library(dplyr)

data(penguins)

penguins_clean <- penguins |> na.omit()

ggpairs(penguins_clean, columns = 3:6, mapping = aes(color = species, alpha = 0.5))








The scatterplots of flipper length vs. body mass and bill length vs. bill depthshow strong separation between speccies. In particular, Gentoo penguins tend to have a larger body mass and flipper length, which makes them more clearly distinguishable. The scatterplots with bill length and bill depth show strong separation between Adelie and Chisntrap species. There are some plots, (ex: jsut body mass or flipper length), show more overlap between Adelie and Chinstrap penguins, which suggests weaker separation in those cases.


Part b (4 points)
Run a scaled principal component analysis on the four quantitative variables using the prcomp() function. How much of the total variation in these variables is explained by the first two principal components? You can use the summary() function on your PCA to find this easily.

penguins_scaled <- scale(penguins_clean[, 3:6])

penguins_pca <- prcomp(penguins_scaled)

summary(penguins_pca)

Importance of components:
                          PC1    PC2     PC3     PC4
Standard deviation     1.6569 0.8821 0.60716 0.32846
Proportion of Variance 0.6863 0.1945 0.09216 0.02697
Cumulative Proportion  0.6863 0.8809 0.97303 1.00000


The first two PCs explain about 88% of the total variation in the data.


Part c (6 points)
Plot the first two principal components with PC1 on the x-axis and PC2 on the y-axis. Color the points by species and comment on how well the first two principal components are separating by species.

pca_df <- as.data.frame(penguins_pca$x)
pca_df$species <- penguins_clean$species

ggplot(pca_df, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = species)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "PCA of Penguins Data")








The first two principal components give pretty good separation between species. Gentoo penguins are clearly separated from the other two species, mostly along PC1. Adelie and Chinstrap penguins show a bit overlap, though there’ss still partial separation along PC2. PCA captures most of the structure in the data, but doesn’t perfectly separate all species.


Part d (12 points)
Now use the umap() function in the umap library to fit a UMAP to the scaled quantitative variables in the penguins dataset. You can use the scale() function to scale the variables. Make sure the UMAP gives two components and set a random_state in the function so your results are reproducible. Then plot the UMAP using the the umap_plot() function in math3190package and color the points by species using the color_umap argument. Experiment with at least three different number of neighbors (n_neighbors) and at least three different minimum distances (min_dist).

library(umap)
library(math3190package)

X_scaled <- scale(penguins_clean[, 3:6])

umap1 <- umap(X_scaled, n_neighbors = 5, min_dist = 0.1, random_state = 3190)
umap_plot(umap1, data = penguins_clean, color_umap = "species")

umap_plot(umap2, data = penguins_clean, color_umap = "species")

umap_plot(umap3, data = penguins_clean, color_umap = "species")










Part e (5 points)
Comment on the UMAP plots you made in part (d). Include a comparison to the PCA plot regarding how well these species are separated.
The UMAP plots usually show better separation between species compared to the PCA plot. Specifically, UMAP typicall forms more distinct clusters. Smaller values of n_neighbors and min_dist produce tighter and more separated clusters, while larger values produce more spread-out and overlapping clusters. Compared to PCA, UMAP can show a nonlinear relationship in the data, which can result in better separation between species. The quality of separation depends on the choice of parameters.
Feel free to repeat this problem using a variable other than species! Sometimes the UMAP outperforms PCA at separating classes, sometimes it does a little worse, and sometimes there are both quite bad.

title: MATH 3190 Homework 4
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_4 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.
Some of the parts in this homework, like 1a, 2b, 2c, 3a, and 3b require writing down some math-heavy expressions. You may either type it up using LaTeX-style formatting in Quarto (preferred), or you can write it by hand (neatly) and include pictures or scans of your work in your Quarto document.

Problem 1 (33 points)
Suppose we want to find the value of \(x\) and the objection function value for finding the global maximum and minimum of the function \(f(x)=\ln(1+x^2)(1+x)e^{-x^2}\). We will implement gradient descent and Newton’s method for finding these extrema.

Part a (5 points)
Find \(f'(x)\) for this function. Do this by hand as a nice derivative refresher. You don’t need to include all of your work, though. Feel free to check your answer using something like Wolfram|Alpha. Write down the derivative here.
\[
f'(x) = e^{-x^2}[\dfrac{2x(1+x)}{1+x^2}+ln(1+x^2)-2x(1+x)ln(1+x)]
\]


Part b (4 points)
Below is the code of a function that implements gradient descent using a backtracking line search each iteration to find \(\gamma_k\): the step size. This function takes eight inputs:

The starting value (or vector)
The function to optimize
The function’s derivative (or gradient)
A logical indicating if we want to find a max with a default of FALSE.
The maximum number of iterations with a default of 200
The initial step size with a default of 1
The line search beta parameter with a default of 0.5.
The stopping tolerance with a default of 1e-10.

The output of this function is a list with the \(x\) value (or vector) that optimizes the function, the function value at that point, and the number of iterations it took to converge. This function works in the case of one dimension or multiple dimensions since you’ll use it again in problem 2.
Take a few minutes to study this function to make sure you understand how it works and what it is doing. Write a few sentences explaining how this function works.

gradient_desc <- function(starting, f, gradient, max = F, maxit = 200, 
                          step_size = 1, line_parameter = 0.5, tol = 1e-10) {
  if(max == T) {
    obj_func <- function(x) {
      -f(x)
    }
    func_deriv <- function(x) {
      -gradient(x)
    }
  } else {
    obj_func <- f
    func_deriv <- gradient
  }
  xk <- starting
  for(k in 1:maxit) {
    gamma <- step_size
    line_beta <- line_parameter
    # Below is the backtracking line search
    while(obj_func(xk - gamma * func_deriv(xk)) > 
          obj_func(xk) - gamma/4 * sum(func_deriv(xk)^2)) {
      gamma <- line_beta * gamma
    }
    # Once we have a good step size, continue
    xnew <- xk - gamma * func_deriv(xk)
    if(norm(xk - xnew, type = "2") < tol) {
      break
    }
    xk <- xnew
    }
  return(list(x = xk, function_val = f(xk), iters = k))
}

This function uses gradient descent to find a minimum or maximum of a function. At each step, it moves in the opposite direction of the gradient. It uses backtracking to shrink the step size until the function decreases enough. The process stops when the change in x is very small or when it reaches the maximum number of iterations.


Part c (5 points)
Use the gradient descent function to find the global min of \(f(x)=\ln(1+x^2)(1+x)e^{-x^2}\). Try using several starting points. Keep track of and report the number of iterations needed to converge at the different starting points.

f <- function(x) {
  log(1 + x^2) * (1+x) * exp(-x^2)
}

fprime <- function(x) {
  exp(-x^2) * (
    (2*x*(1+x))/(1 + x^2) + 
    log(1+x^2) -
    2*x*(1+x)*log(1+x^2)
    )
}

Part e (8 points)
Use the fact that \[
f''(x)=\dfrac{2e^{-x^2}}{(1+x^2)^2}\bigg((2x^3+2x^2-3x-1)(1+x^2)^2\ln(1+x^2)-4x^5-4x^4-3x^3-5x^2+3x+1\bigg)
\] to implement Newton’s method to find the global max and the global min. Use the same starting points you did in parts c and d. Keep track of and report the number of iterations needed to converge at the different starting points. And yes, that second derivative is very messy! This is one reason why Newton’s method is less popular. You don’t have to write a function for this part to implement Newton’s method, but you can if you’d like.

fsecond <- function(x) {
  (2*exp(-x^2) / (1 + x^2)^2) *
    ((2*x^3 + 2*x^2 - 3*x - 1)*(1 + x^2)^2*log(1 + x^2)
     - 4*x^5 - 4*x^4 - 3*x^3 - 5*x^2 + 3*x + 1)
}

newtons_method <- function(starting, tol = 1e-10, maxit = 200) {
  xk <- starting
  
  for(k in 1:maxit) {
    xnew <- xk - fprime(xk)/fsecond(xk)
    
    if(abs(xnew - xk) < tol) {
      break
      
    }
    
    xk <- xnew
  }
  
  return(list(x = xk,
              function_val = f(xk),
              iters = k))
}

Part f (3 points)
Compare the number of iterations needed for convergence for gradient descent and for Newton’s method.
In general, Newton’s method requires less iterations to converge than gradient descent. Newton’s method uses first and second derivatives, which helps it more accurately approximate the critical point and converge faster. Gradient descent only uses the first derivative, so it usually takes more iterations to converge.

Problem 2 (26 points)
Suppose \(\boldsymbol{x} = (x_1,\dots, x_n )^T\) are independent and identically distributed random variables with probability density function (pdf) given by \[
f(x_i|\theta) = \theta(1-x_i)^{\theta-1};\ \ 0\le x_i< 1,\ 0<\theta<\infty,\ i=1,\dots,n
\]

Part a (4 points)
Using ggplot, plot the probability density function (pdf) for an individual \(x_i\) given \(\theta=0.5\) and then again for \(\theta=5\).

pdf_fun <- function(x, theta) {
  theta * (1-x)^(theta-1)
}

library(ggplot2)

x_vals <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 1000)

df <- data.frame(
  x = rep(x_vals, 2),
  density = c(pdf_fun(x_vals, 0.5),
              pdf_fun(x_vals, 5)),
  theta = factor(rep(c(0.5, 5), each = length(x_vals)))
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = density, color = theta)) +
  geom_line(size = 1) +
  labs(title = "PDF of f(x | θ)",
    y = "Density",
    color = expression(theta)) +
  theme_minimal()

Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.











Part b (5 points)
Give the likelihood \(L(\theta|\boldsymbol{x})\) and log-likelihood \(\ell(\theta|\boldsymbol{x})\) functions in this situation.
Likelihood function: \[
L(\theta \mid \mathbf{x}) = \theta^n \prod_{i=1}^n (1 - x_i)^{\theta - 1}
\] Log-likelihood function: \[
\ell(\theta \mid \mathbf{x}) = n \log \theta + (\theta - 1)\sum_{i=1}^n \log(1 - x_i)
\]


Part c (5 points)
Find the Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE) \(\hat{\theta}\) for \(\theta\).
\[
\hat{\theta} = \frac{n} {-\sum_{i=1}^n \log(1 - x_i)}
\]


Part d (3 points)
Show that your estimator is in fact a maximum. That is, check the second derivative of the log-likelihood.

set.seed(3190)
n <- 10
theta_true <- 2
x <- 1 - runif(n)^(1/theta_true)

loglik <- function(theta, x) {
  n <- length(x)
  n * log(theta) + (theta - 1) * sum(log(1-x))
}

dloglik <- function(theta, x) {
  n <- length(x)
  n/theta + sum(log(1 - x))
}

d2loglik <- function(theta, x) {
  n <- length(x)
  -n / theta^2
}

theta_hat <- n / (-sum(log(1 - x)))

theta_hat

Part e (2 points)
Find the MLE if the data values are given below. Note, the actual \(\theta\) value used to generate these data was \(\theta=7.3\).

x <- c(0.0194, 0.0053, 0.2488, 0.0456, 0.2059, 0.0992, 0.1168, 0.3705, 
       0.2129, 0.018, 0.0464, 0.1401, 0.0759, 0.1588, 0.0334, 0.2931, 
       0.0662, 0.2292, 0.1581, 0.3462, 0.035, 0.1086, 0.0793, 0.2095, 
       0.1419, 0.1835, 0.1107, 0.0764, 0.1331, 0.042, 0.0911, 0.1608)

Part f (7 points)
To get an idea of how variable the maximum likelihood estimator is in this case, let’s generate many samples of size 32 from this distribution (which is a Beta distribution with parameters \(1\) and \(\theta\), but you don’t really need to know that). We can do this by using rbeta(32, 1, 7.3).
Using the code below, generate at least 10,000 samples this way, compute the MLE for each, and then plot a histogram of the values using ggplot. Plot a vertical line at the true \(\theta\) value of 7.3 (you can use geom_vline(xintercept = 7.3) for this). On the plot somewhere, indicate what the standard deviation in those estimates is. Of course, this code is incomplete, so you’ll have to complete it. Once you do, make sure to change the eval option to true.
Once you’ve completed the code for the samples of size 32, repeat this but instead of taking samples of size 32, take samples of size 100. How does this change the histogram, where the distribution is centered, and the standard deviation of the estimates, if at all?

library(ggplot2)
set.seed(2026)
theta_hat <- rep(0, 10000) # Initialize the vector
for(i in 1:10000) {
  x_sim <- rbeta(32, 1, 7.3)
  theta_hat[i] <- 32 / (-sum(log(1 - x_sim)))
}

theta_sd <- sd(theta_hat)

ggplot(data.frame(theta_hat), aes(x = theta_hat)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "skyblue", color = "darkgreen") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 7.3, color = "purple", linetype = "dashed", size = 1) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of MLEs for θ (n=32, 10,000 simulations)",
       x = expression(hat(theta)),
       y = "Frequency",
       subtitle = paste("Std. dev. of estimates =", round(theta_sd, 3))) +
  theme_minimal()











Problem 3 (31 points)
We implemented optimization to maximize the likelihood for a Poisson regression problem in the notes to estimate the \(\beta\) (intercept and slope) values. Let’s do something similar for find the \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) vector by maximizing the likelihood in logistic regression.
In logistic regression, we attempt to predict the probability of a success for a given case. We can use the Bernoulli random variable for this. For a Bernoulli random variable, \(P(Y=y_i) = p_i^{y_i}(1-p_i)^{1-y_i}\) for \(y_i=0,1\) where \(p_i\) is the probability of a success. In our case, for logistic regression, we have \(\text{logit}(p_i)=\ln\left(\dfrac{p_i}{1-p_i}\right)=\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta}\). That means \(p_i = \dfrac{\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}{1+\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}\).
The likelihood function, \(L(\boldsymbol{\beta}|\mathbf{X},\boldsymbol{y})\), for the logistic regression for a sample of size \(n\) is \[\begin{align*}
L(\boldsymbol{\beta}|\mathbf{X},\boldsymbol{y}) &=\prod_{i=1}^n p_i^{y_i}(1-p_i)^{1-y_i}\\
&=\prod_{i=1}^n\left[\dfrac{\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}{1+\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}\right]^{y_i}\left(1-\left[\dfrac{\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}{1+\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}\right]\right)^{1-y_i}
\end{align*}\]

Part a (7 points)
Using the above likelihood, show that the log likelihood function is \[
\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}|\mathbf{X},\boldsymbol{y})=\sum_{i=1}^ny_i\ln\left(\dfrac{\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}{1+\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}\right) + \sum_{i=1}^n(1-y_i)\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{1+\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}\right)
\] and then show it can be equivalently written \[
\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}|\mathbf{X},\boldsymbol{y}) = \sum_{i=1}^ny_i(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta}) - \sum_{i=1}^n\ln\Big(1 + \exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})\Big)
\] using properties of logarithms.

# Logistic regression log-likelihood function
# X: design matrix (n x p), y: response vector (0/1), beta: parameter vector (p x 1)
loglik_logistic <- function(beta, X, y) {
  eta <- X %*% beta              # linear predictor
  sum(y * eta - log(1 + exp(eta)))  # log-likelihood
}

# Example usage:
# Suppose we have a dataset with an intercept and one predictor
# X <- cbind(1, x)  # add intercept column
# y <- c(0,1,0,1,...)  # response vector
# beta_start <- rep(0, ncol(X))  # initial guess for beta
# loglik_logistic(beta_start, X, y)



Part b (6 points)
Find the gradient of the log likelihood if we have three \(\beta\)’s. That is, if \(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta}=\beta_0+\beta_1x_{i1}+\beta_2x_{i2}\).
\[
\eta_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{i1} + \beta_2 x_{i2}
\]
\[
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \beta_j} = \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - p_i) x_{ij}, \quad j = 0,1,2
\]
where (x_{i0} = 1) for the intercept and
\[
p_i = \frac{\exp(\eta_i)}{1 + \exp(\eta_i)}
\]
\[
\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}) =
\begin{bmatrix}
\sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - p_i) \\
\sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - p_i) x_{i1} \\
\sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - p_i) x_{i2}
\end{bmatrix}
\]


Part c (4 points)
In Homework 3, Problem 2, part a, you read in the adult dataset (from the UC Irvine database). This is the one that we used to predict whether a person makes over $50K a year based on some other variables. The data came from the Census Bureau in 1994 and can be found in the Data folder in my Math3190_S26 GitHub repo. More info on the dataset can be found in the “adult.names” file.
Read in the dataset and put column names like you did in HW 3. Then fit a logistic regression model using the glm() function that predicts salary from age/10 and sex. Note, we should divide the age by 10 in our model because this makes the gradient descent more stable (for whatever reason). We can divide by 10 in the glm() function by wrapping it in the I() function. Like this: glm(salary ~ I(age/10) + sex, data = adult, family = binomial).

adult <- read.csv("/Users/janewillden/Downloads/adult.data", header = FALSE)

colnames(adult) <- c("age", "workclass", "fnlwgt", "education", "education_num",
                     "marital_status", "occupation", "relationship", "race", "sex",
                     "capital_gain", "capital_loss", "hours_per_week", "native_country",
                     "salary")

model <- glm(I(salary) == ">50K" ~ I(age/10) + sex, data = adult, family = binomial)

summary(model)

Define y, x1, and x2. y should be a vector of 0’s and 1’s with a 1 indicating that the person had a salary above $50,000. x1 should be a vector that contains all of the age values (divided by 10) and then x2 should be an indicator variable that indicates if the person is male or not. y <- ifelse(adult$salary == ">50K", 1, 0)

Part e (7 points)
Use the gradient descent function from problem 1 to find estimates for \(\beta_0\), \(\beta_1\), and \(\beta_2\) for predicting salary from age and sex. Enter the following in your function:

Use the vector c(0, 0, 0) as your starting point.
Instead of starting the step size, \(\gamma_k\), at 1, start it at 0.1 to save some time with the line search.
Make sure the maximum number of iterations is at least 200.

You’ll know you did this right if the optimized values you end up with match (or are very close to) the estimates from your logistic regression model you fit in part c. This may take a minute or two to run, so you may want to cache this code chunk.

gradient_desc <- function(starting, gradient, maxit = 200, step_size = 0.1, tol = 1e-10) {
  xk <- starting
  for(k in 1:maxit) {
    gamma <- step_size
    xnew <- xk + gamma * gradient(xk)
    if(norm(xnew - xk, type = "2") < tol) break
    xk <- xnew
  }
  return(list(beta = xnew, iters = k))
}

loglik_gradient <- function(beta) {
  eta <- X %*% beta
  p <- 1 / (1 + exp(-eta))
  t(X) %*% (y - p)
}

beta_start <- c(0, 0, 0)

gd_result <- gradient_desc(starting = beta_start,
                           gradient = loglik_gradient,
                           maxit = 200,
                           step_size = 0.1)

The results here should also be very close to the numbers we obtained for the slopes in the model we fit in part c, but may not match exactly due to different stopping criteria.

loglik_logistic <- function(beta, X, y) {
  eta <- X %*% beta
  sum(y * eta - log(1 + exp(eta)))
}

X <- cbind(1, x1, x2)

beta_start <- c(0, 0, 0)

optim_result <- optim(par = beta_start,

                      hessian = TRUE)

title: MATH 3190 Homework 8
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_8 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

Problem 1 (10 points)
Three airlines serve a small town in Ohio. Airline A has 52% of all scheduled flights, airline B has 35% and airline C has the remaining 13%. Their on-time rates are 85%, 67%, and 41%, respectively. A flight just left on-time. What is the probability that it was a flight of airline A?
The probability of a flight being on time:
\(P(T) = P(A)P(T|A) + P(B)P(T|B) + P(C)P(T|C) = (0.52*0.85) + (0.35*0.67) + (0.13*0.41) = 0.7298\)
The probability of a flight being on time from airline A:
\(P(A|T) = \dfrac{P(A)P(T|A)}{P(T)} = \dfrac{0.52 * 0.85}{0.7298} = 0.605645\)


Problem 2 (15 points)
Suppose we have a data set with each observation \(x_i\) independent and identically exponentially distributed for \(i=1,2,\dots,n\). That is, \(x_i\sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)\) where \(\lambda\) is the rate parameter. We would like to find a posterior (or at least a function proportional to it) for \(\lambda\).

Part a (6 points)
Write down the likelihood function (or a function proportional to it) in this situation. We would call this \(p(x|\lambda)\).
\(p(x|\lambda) \propto \lambda^n e^{-\lambda \sum_{i=1}^n x_i}\)


Part b (6 points)
Now let \(\lambda\) have a normal prior with mean 0.1 and variance 1: \(\lambda\sim N(1/10,\ 1)\). Use this and the likelihood from part (a) to write down a function that is proportional to the posterior of \(\lambda\) given \(\boldsymbol{x}\). We call this \(p(\lambda|\boldsymbol{x})\).
\(p(\lambda|x) \propto \lambda^n \exp\left(-\lambda \sum_{i=1}^n x_i - \frac{(\lambda - 0.1)^2}{2}\right)\)


Part c (3 points)
Which would be more appropriate here to obtain samples of \(\lambda\), the Gibbs or Metropolis algorithm? Explain why. You may want to look on page 171 of Notes 9 in the conjugate prior table.
The Metropolis Algorithm would be more appropriate here. This is because the prior for \(\lambda\) is normal, but the likelihood comes from an exponential distribution, which is why the posterior isn’t standard form to the prior. The Metropolis algorithm can sample from any posterior distribution up to a proportionality constant, while Gibbs sampling requires knowing the full conditional distributions in closed form and being able to sample from them directly.



Problem 3 (30 points)
Suppose we have the vector x = c(1.83, 1.72, 2.13, 2.49, 0.90, 2.01, 1.51, 3.12, 1.29, 1.54, 2.94, 3.02, 0.93, 2.78) that we believe comes from a gamma distribution with shape of 10 and some rate \(\beta\): \(x_i\sim\text{Gam}(10,\ \beta)\). We will use sampling to obtain some information about \(\beta\). Let’s put a gamma prior on \(\beta\) with a shape of \(\alpha_0\) and a rate of 1: \(\beta\sim\text{Gam}(\alpha_0,\ 1)\).

Part a (5 points)
Use the fact that this is a conjugate prior to write down what kind of distribution the posterior of \(\beta\), which is \(p(\beta|\boldsymbol{x})\), is.
Gamma with shape \(\alpha_0 + 10n\) and rate \(\beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^n x_1\)


Part b (5 points)
Let \(\alpha_0=1\). In an R code chunk, sample 10,000 \(\beta\) values from the distribution you wrote down in part (a) using the rgamma() function and report the 95% credible interval for \(\beta\) using the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles.

x = c(1.83, 1.72, 2.13, 2.49, 0.90, 2.01, 1.51, 3.12, 1.29, 1.54, 2.94, 3.02, 0.93, 2.78)
shape1 <- 1 + length(x)*10
rate <- 1 + sum(x)
beta1 <- rgamma(10000, shape1, rate)
quantile(beta1, c(0.025, 0.97))

    2.5%      97% 
4.070269 5.601357 




Part c (3 points)
Repeat part (b) with \(\alpha_0 = 10\).

x = c(1.83, 1.72, 2.13, 2.49, 0.90, 2.01, 1.51, 3.12, 1.29, 1.54, 2.94, 3.02, 0.93, 2.78)
shape2 <- 10 + length(x)*10
rate <- 1 + sum(x)
beta2 <- rgamma(10000, shape2, rate)
quantile(beta2, c(0.025, 0.97))

    2.5%      97% 
4.356150 5.948646 




Part d (3 points)
Repeat part (b) with \(\alpha_0 = 100\).

x = c(1.83, 1.72, 2.13, 2.49, 0.90, 2.01, 1.51, 3.12, 1.29, 1.54, 2.94, 3.02, 0.93, 2.78)

quantile(beta3, c(0.025, 0.97))

    2.5%      97% 
7.217000 9.225369 




Part e (4 points)
Comment on the effect the value of \(\alpha_0\) has on the credible intervals for \(\beta\) as revealed in parts (b), (c), and (d).
The higher value of \(\alpha_0\), the wider the credible interval is. With an \(\alpha_0\) of 1, the credible interval is about 4.06 to 5.64. For an \(\alpha_0\) of 10, the credible interval is about 4.36 to 5.94. For an \(\alpha_0\) of 100, the credible interval is about 7.21 to 9.32.


Part f (6 points)
Now suppose we have twice as much data (given in the R code chunk below). Repeat parts (b), (c), and (d) using this x vector given below instead of the other one and report the three 95% credible intervals. Note, this new vector x will change the shape and rate parameters used in the rgamma() functions.

x <- c(1.83, 1.72, 2.13, 2.49, 0.90, 2.01, 1.51, 3.12, 1.29, 1.54,
       2.94, 3.02, 0.93, 2.78, 2.76, 1.70, 1.42, 2.16, 1.07, 2.21,
       2.38, 2.27, 1.72, 1.44, 1.54, 1.72, 1.87, 1.39)

quantile(beta2, c(0.025, 0.97))

2.296501 2.881229 

shape3 <- 100 + length(x*2)*10
rate <- 1 + sum(x*2)
beta3 <- rgamma(10000, shape1, rate)
quantile(beta1, c(0.025, 0.97))

    2.5%      97% 
2.286472 2.880246 




Part g (4 points)
In this problem, the true \(\beta\) value is 5. Write a sentence or two about the effect adding more data has to these credible intervals by comparing the intervals from parts (b)-(d) to the intervals from part (f).
When the amount of sample data is increased, a more accurate estimate of the true beta value can be achieved because as the sample size increases, the confidence intervals become narrower and more definite.



Problem 4 (35 points)
Let’s apply the Bayesian framework to a regression problem. In the math3190package library is the treeseeds dataset (it is also on GitHub) that contains information about species of tree, the count of seeds it produces, and the average weight of those seeds in mg.

Part a (3 points)
Using the treeseeds data, create new variables defined by taking the log of the counts and weights. Fit an OLS regression model using logged weight to predict logged count.

library(math3190package) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
tree <- treeseeds |>
  mutate(lcount = log(count),
         lweight = log(weight))

model <- lm(lcount ~ lweight, data = tree)
summary(model)


Call:
lm(formula = lcount ~ lweight, data = tree)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-2.4429 -0.3877  0.1778  0.6167  1.2691 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  9.37903    0.39720  23.613 1.95e-14 ***
lweight     -0.51491    0.07185  -7.166 1.58e-06 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.9363 on 17 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.7513,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7367 
F-statistic: 51.35 on 1 and 17 DF,  p-value: 1.58e-06




Part b (5 points)
Please read this carefully and try to understand what is being done here. There is just one question at the end of this part for you. We will walk through the mathematics and code of obtaining the posterior and samples together here since this problem will focus on looking at diagnostics, but I want you to see the Metropolis algorithm in action! Make sure to read this in the html or pdf file where all equations are rendered nicely.
Assuming the true errors are normal with mean 0 and variance \(\sigma^2\), \(\epsilon_i\sim N(0,\ \sigma^2)\), it can be shown that each \(y_i\) has the distribution \[
p(y_i|x_i,\beta_0,\beta_1\sigma^2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\exp\left(
-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}(y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1x_i)^2\right)
\]
So, we can write the likelihood is \[
p(y_i|x_i,\beta_0,\beta_1\sigma^2)\propto\exp\left(
-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}(y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1x_i)^2\right)
\] where \(y_i\) is the log(count) for observation \(i\) and \(x_i\) is the log(weight) for observation \(i\). Note that here we think of \(\boldsymbol{y}\) as being random and \(\boldsymbol{x}\) as being fixed. We could, in theory, think of the vector \(\boldsymbol{x}\) as also being random and put a prior on it. But we won’t do that here.
Now, let’s just put uniform priors on \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) so the priors are proportional to 1. Also, let’s assume \(\sigma^2=1\). This seems reasonable since \(s_e^2\), the MSE, is 0.877. Of course, we could put a prior on \(\sigma^2\) as well and sample it too, but we will focus on only sampling \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\).
Now, with those uniform priors, and plugging in 1 for \(\sigma^2\), we have that the joint posterior of \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) is: \[
p(\beta_0,\beta_1|\boldsymbol{x},\boldsymbol{y}) \propto\exp\left(
-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1x_i)^2\right)=f(\beta_0,\beta_1|\boldsymbol{x},\boldsymbol{y}).
\]
Then, we can take the log to get \[
\ln(f(\beta_0,\beta_1|\boldsymbol{x},\boldsymbol{y}))=-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1x_i)^2.
\]
Our goal now is to obtain samples of \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\). Let’s use the Metropolis algorithm to do this. Using the log of the function proportional to the joint posterior of \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\), \(\ln\Big(f(\beta_0,\beta_1|\boldsymbol{x},\boldsymbol{y})\Big)\), we’ll write a Metropolis algorithm in R. For \(\beta_0\), we will use a normal proposal distribution centered at the previous value, \(\beta_0^{(i)}\), with a standard deviation of 0.8 and for \(\beta_1\), and we will use a normal proposal distribution centered at the previous value, \(\beta_1^{(i)}\), with a standard deviation of 0.1. The starting values don’t matter too much, but we’ll use \(\beta_0^{(0)}=10\) and \(\beta_1^{(0)}=-0.5\).
This code will generate 10,000 samples and plot the chains for \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\). In addition, the 95% credible interval for \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) are given.

library(patchwork)
set.seed(2026)
nsamps <- 10000
beta0 <- rep(0, nsamps)
beta1 <- rep(0, nsamps)
beta0[1] <- 10
beta1[1] <- -0.5
for (i in 1:(nsamps - 1)) {
  beta0_star <- rnorm(1, beta0[i], 0.8) # Proposed new value for intercept
  beta1_star <- rnorm(1, beta1[i], 0.1) # Proposed new value for slope
  # y is tree$lcount and x is tree$lweight
  lf1 <- -1 / 2 * sum((tree$lcount - beta0_star - beta1_star * tree$lweight)^2)
  lf2 <- -1 / 2 * sum((tree$lcount - beta0[i] - beta1[i] * tree$lweight)^2)
  if (log(runif(1)) < lf1 - lf2) {
    # Accept the proposed values as new sample values if the above 
    #   condition is satisfied
    beta0[i + 1] <- beta0_star
    beta1[i + 1] <- beta1_star
  } else {
    # Otherwise set the new sample values to be equal to the previous ones
    beta0[i + 1] <- beta0[i]
    beta1[i + 1] <- beta1[i]
  }
}

mcmc_df <- data.frame(index = 1:nsamps, beta0 = beta0, beta1 = beta1)
mcmc_plot1 <- 
  ggplot(mcmc_df, aes(x = index, y = beta0)) +
  geom_line() +
  theme_test() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 10000, by = 2000)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(8, 11, by = 0.5)) + 
  labs(x = "Index", y = expression(beta[0]))

mcmc_plot2 <- 
  ggplot(mcmc_df, aes(x = index, y = beta1)) +
  geom_line() +
  theme_test() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 10000, by = 2000)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(-0.75, -0.25, by = 0.1)) + 
  labs(x = "Index", y = expression(beta[1]))

mcmc_plot1 / mcmc_plot2







quantile(beta0, c(.025, .975))

     2.5%     97.5% 
 8.526416 10.210557 

quantile(beta1, c(.025, .975))

      2.5%      97.5% 
-0.6606829 -0.3681291 


The plots of the chains are given in the output above.
The 95% credible interval for \(\beta_0\) is \((8.53, 10.21)\).
The 95% credible interval for \(\beta_1\) is \((-0.661, -0.368)\).
Your intervals may differ a bit due to the random sampling.
Okay, here is the question for you! Based on the plots of the chains above, does it look like the Metropolis sampling worked fairly well? Explain.
It does look like the Metropolis sampling worked fairly well. Both \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) fluctuate around a stable region with no apparent trend, which suggests convergence. The chains also show good mixing. They move often instead of getting stucki, which suggests the algorithm is effectively sampling from the posterior distribution.


Part c (3 points)
Interpret both of the credible intervals from part (b).
The 95% credible interval for \(\beta_0\) means that given the data and model, there’s a 95% probability that the true intercept lies between 8.53 and 10.21. The 95% credible interval for \(\beta_1\) means that there is a 95% probability that the true slope lies between -0.661 and -0.368. This indicates a negative relationship.


Part d (5 points)
Find and report the integrated autocorrelation time for the \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) chains. Each chain will have their own \(\hat{\tau}_{int}\) value, so you should report two (although they will be similar).

library(coda)

tau_int_beta0 <- 10000 / effectiveSize(as.mcmc(beta0))
tau_int_beta1 <- 10000 / effectiveSize(as.mcmc(beta1))

tau_int_beta0

    var1 
16.78732 

tau_int_beta1

    var1 
22.04703 




Part e (3 points)
Based on the integrated autocorrelation time for the \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) chains, how many MCMC samples would you need to generate to get the equivalent of 10,000 independent samples?
For \(\beta_0\), you would need about 167,873 samples. For \(\beta_1\), you would need about 220,470 samples.


Part f (3 points)
Let’s compare these credible intervals to some other intervals. First, obtain the 95% \(t\) confidence intervals for \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) just using the confint() function on the model you fit in part (a) and report them here.

model <- lm(lcount ~ lweight, data = tree)
confint(model, level = 0.95)

                 2.5 %     97.5 %
(Intercept)  8.5410148 10.2170375
lweight     -0.6665051 -0.3633051


The 95% t-based interval for \(\beta_0\) is (8.54, 10.22). The 95% t-based interval for \(\beta_1\) is (-0.67, -0.36).


Part g (8 points)
Now let’s obtain confidence intervals using bootstrapping the cases like we did with in Notes 7 and HW 5. You can use the bootstrapping() function in the math3190package for this or do it yourself with a loop. Set a seed and then using at least 10,000 bootstrap samples, report the 95% percentile confidence intervals for \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\).

library(math3190package) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
set.seed(2026)

reg_fun <- function(data, i) {
  lm(lcount ~ lweight, data = data[i, ])$coef
}

boot_out <- bootstrapping(tree, reg_fun, 10000)

boot_ci(boot_out)

Warning in boot.ci(boot_out, conf, type, index = index, ...): bootstrap
variances needed for studentized intervals
Warning in boot.ci(boot_out, conf, type, index = index, ...): bootstrap

Part i (5 points)
Write a couple sentences comparing all of the intervals in parts (b), (f), and (g).
The confidence intervals in parts (b), (f), and (g) are all fairly similar for both \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\). This would be expected since the model assumptions are reasonable and the priors were flat.

text:

Problem 1 (70 points)
Before we begin, please re-install the math3190package since there was an update to the classifier_plot() function that is needed on this assignment. Use either devtools::install_github("rbrown53/math3190package") or
remotes::install_github("rbrown53/math3190package").
The National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases conducted a study on 768 adult female Pima Native Americans living near Phoenix. The purpose of the study was to investigate factors related to diabetes. The response variable in this study (test) was whether or not the patient showed signs of diabetes (1 = yes, 0 = no). One explanatory variable considered was the plasma glucose concentration (in mg/dL) at 2 hours in an oral glucose tolerance test (glucose). Many other potential explanatory variables were considered as well including body mass index (BMI). The goal here is to predict the diabetes status of the subject, which is in the results variable.

Part a (5 points)
Install and/or load the faraway package with the pima dataset. Set a seed and then split the data into a testing and a training set with the testing set containing 20% of the data values. When you do this, change the name of the variable “test” to “result” since it will be a bit confusing to call that test when we have a test dataset. Then make the result variable a factor so R treats it as categorical rather than numeric.

library(faraway)
library(caret) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
library(dplyr) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
set.seed(3190)

data(pima)
pima <- pima |>
  rename(result = test)

pima$result <- as.factor(pima$result)

train_index <- createDataPartition(pima$result, p = 0.8, list = FALSE)

pima_train <- pima[train_index, ]
pima_test <- pima[-train_index, ]



Part b (6 points)

Fit a logistic regression model to the training set for predicting result from the glucose and bmi variables.
Then use the classifier_plot() function in the math3190package (make sure you re-installed it!) to make a plot that shows the regions on the glucose-bmi-plane that are predicted to be 0 and 1. This shows how logistic regression divides the classes. Use the training set as the data input in this function.
Next, obtain the predictions for the test set with a probability cutoff of 0.5 to predict if the person has diabetes. Create a confusion matrix using the confusionMatrix() function in the caret library. Make sure to put positive = "1" for this and all other confusion matrices on this assignment. Print all of the output from that function that shows the confusion matrix and many associated statistics.
Write a sentence or two discussing how well this model performs in predicting on the test set.


library(math3190package) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()

pima_mod = glm(result ~ glucose + bmi, data = pima_train, family = "binomial")
classifier_plot(pima_mod, pima_train)







probability = predict(pima_mod, newdata = pima_test, type = "response")
predictions = ifelse(probability > 0.5, 1, 0)
predictions = as.factor(predictions)
confusionMatrix(predictions, pima_test$result, positive = "1")

This model performs with an accuracy of about 76%, which is pretty good. It has a strong specificity of 0.88, which means it pretty effectively identifies individuals without diabetes, but has a moderate sensitivity of 0.55, which means that it fails to detect diabetes in some individuals. Overall, the model seems to perform fairly well; it’s more reliable for predciting the absence of diabetes rather than the presence of diabetes.


Part c (3 points)
Now let’s use naïve Bayes to get predictions. Use the naive_bayes() function in the naivebayes library to fit a model predicting result using glucose and bmi. Find the predicted probabilities of being a 0 and a 1 for the first observation in the training set.

library(naivebayes) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
nb_mod <- naive_bayes(result ~ glucose + bmi, data = pima_train)

Part d (9 points)
Now find the predicted probability of being a 0 and a 1 for the first observation in the training set “by hand” like we did in the notes on pages 187 and 188. To simplify this a bit, let’s again only use the variables glucose and bmi here. Of course, use R to help you (the dnorm() function and some tidyverse functions will be especially nice), but don’t use the naive_bayes() function here. Note: these variables we are using to predict are numeric, so we will assume a normal distribution for both of them (maybe not a good assumption, but let’s go with it). After you get the probabilities, compare them to the ones you got in part (c). They should match.

x = pima_train[1, ]
p0 = mean(pima_train$result == 0)
p1 = mean(pima_train$result == 1)

t0 = filter(pima_train, result == 0)
t1 = filter(pima_train, result == 1)

g0_mean = mean(t0$glucose)
g0_sd = sd(t0$glucose)
b0_mean = mean(t0$bmi)
b0_sd = sd(t0$bmi)

g1_mean = mean(t1$glucose)
g1_sd = sd(t1$glucose)
b1_mean = mean(t1$bmi)
b1_sd = sd(t1$bmi)

l_g0 = dnorm(x$glucose, g0_mean, g0_sd)
l_b0 = dnorm(x$bmi, b0_mean, b0_sd)
l_g1 = dnorm(x$glucose, g1_mean, g1_sd)
l_b1 = dnorm(x$bmi, b1_mean, b1_sd)

num0 = p0 * l_g0 * l_b0
num1 = p1 * l_g1 * l_b1

prob_0 = num0 / (num0 + num1)
prob_1 = num1 / (num0 + num1)

prob_0

[1] 0.4118944

prob_1

[1] 0.5881056


The probabilities were identical here to the probabilities in part c.


Part e (3 points)
Now fit a naïve Bayes model predicting result from glucose and bmi using a kernel instead of assuming a normal distribution. You can do this by setting usekernel = TRUE in the naive_bayes() function.

nb_kernel <- naive_bayes(result ~ glucose + bmi, data = pima_train, usekernel = TRUE)

predict(nb_kernel, type = "prob")[1, ]

        0         1 
0.3885088 0.6114912 




Part f (5 points)
Now use the classifier_plot() function to make two plots: one using the first naïve Bayes from part (c) and one using the naïve Bayes with the kernel from part (e). Both should show the separation on the training set. Comment on how these models seems to perform in separating the results classes and if there seems like there may be any over-fitting here.

classifier_plot(nb_mod, pima_train)







classifier_plot(nb_kernel, pima_train)








There’s a lot of overlapping between the data, but it seems like the first naïve Bayes produces a smoother decision boundary, while the kernel naïve Bayes produces a more flexible and irregular boundary that follows the training data more closely. This suggests that the kernel could be capturing more complex patterns in the data, but it also might be overfitting and fitting noise in the training set as opposite to actual patterns.

Part i (10 points)
Now let’s use a different kernel. Let’s use the radial kernel for the SVM. This has two tuning parameters: the cost and \(\gamma\). Let’s use the train() function in the caret package to do 5-fold cross validation (set a seed, please) to select both of those parameters. However, the train() function uses \(\sigma\) instead of \(\gamma\) and \(C\) instead of cost.
Use the trainControl() function with the trControl option and the tuneGrid option in the train() function to perform this cross-validation. For sigma, use seq(0.1, 2, by = 0.1) and for C also use seq(0.1, 2, by = 0.1). Put method = "svmRadial" in the train() function as well so it knows to use a SVM with the radial kernel. You will need to install the kernlab package for this to work. Set a seed here as well.
By default, the “best” sigma and C values will be chosen with accuracy instead of Cohen’s kappa. Make sure to choose the best values using kappa (although both metrics may choose the same “best” values).

library(kernlab)

library(caret)
set.seed(3190)

svm_radial <- train(result ~ ., method = "svmRadial", data = pima_train, trControl = trainControl(method ="cv", number = 5), tuneGrid = expand.grid(sigma = seq(0.1, 2, by = 0.1), C = seq(0.1, 2, by = 0.1)), metric = "Kappa")

Part j (3 points)
Fit the SVM with the radial kernel using the svm() function and the cross-validated cost and \(\gamma\) found in part (i).

svm_radial_final <- svm(result ~ ., data = pima_train, kernel = "radial", cost = 0.5, gamma = 50, scale = TRUE)



Part k (5 points)
Create the classifier plots for both SVM models and plot the training set and comment on how well these seem to separate the classes.

svm_poly_plot <- svm(result ~ glucose + bmi, data = pima_train, kernel = "polynomial", scale = TRUE)

plot(svm_radial_plot, data = pima_train, glucose ~ bmi)
